Name: quantization_proxies_information_theory
Title: Interpretable Quantization Proxies: Bridging Rate-Distortion Theory and Model Performance
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formalizing the relationship between quantization bit-width and predictive performance through an information-theoretic lens, we can develop interpretable quantization proxies that improve model accuracy while adhering to rate-distortion constraints.
Related Work: While previous works like 'Rate Distortion For Model Compression' (Gao et al., 2018) and 'Radio: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Large Language Model Compression' (Young, 2025) focus on model compression via rate-distortion theory, they often lack a structured approach to establish interpretable proxies for quantization. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by deriving quantization strategies based on mutual information metrics that are directly interpretable in terms of model performance.
Abstract: The compression of neural networks through quantization is critical for deploying models in resource-constrained environments. However, existing quantization strategies often lack interpretability and fail to leverage the full potential of rate-distortion theory. In this proposal, we introduce a novel framework for defining quantization proxies that are grounded in information-theoretic principles. By characterizing the relationship between quantization bit-width, model performance, and task-relevant distortion through mutual information measures, we aim to develop interpretable quantization strategies. We will conduct experiments to validate our framework by evaluating model performance against theoretical rate-distortion limits across various architectures and datasets. Our approach seeks to provide a clear understanding of how quantization impacts predictive accuracy, leading to improved design choices in model compression.
Experiments: 1. Implement quantization strategies based on the proposed information-theoretic framework across several neural network architectures (e.g., CNNs and transformers).
2. Measure the mutual information between model weights and their outputs to establish a baseline for predictive sufficiency.
3. Evaluate the trade-offs between bit-width, model accuracy, and distortion using datasets such as MNIST and CIFAR-10.
4. Compare the performance of our quantization proxies against state-of-the-art methods using metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, and robustness to noise.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the complexity of deriving interpretable proxies that adequately capture performance; limitations may arise from the assumptions made regarding the distribution of model weights and task relevance. Additionally, the generalizability of the proposed framework across different tasks and architectures needs thorough validation.
